q: This won't compile — use after move. What's the reason?

opts.t: s was moved into t; String is not Copy
println! requires a mutable reference
opts.why: println! borrows immutably; mutability is unrelated.

opts.t: String literals can't be bound twice
opts.why: The literal is fine; the issue is ownership transfer, not the literal.

opts.t: t must be the same type as s first
opts.why: Both are String — type identity is not the problem here.

why: Assigning `s` to `t` moves ownership; `String` doesn't implement `Copy`, so `s` is no longer valid after the move (E0382). Fix: borrow with `&s`, or call `s.clone()` to give `t` an independent copy.
fj: "Just use t — s is gone."
fs: "Move = transfer of ownership; borrow or clone to keep both alive."

q: Compiler: E0382 — use of moved value `s`. Pick the fix.

opts.t: Make s mutable: let mut s
opts.why: Mutability doesn't prevent a move; `mut` controls write access, not ownership.

opts.t: Wrap the call in unsafe { }
opts.why: unsafe doesn't bypass move semantics; ownership rules are compile-time.

opts.t: Pass a borrow: takes_ownership(&s)
Use a raw pointer: &raw s
opts.why: Raw pointers bypass safety guarantees and are not the idiomatic fix here.

why: Passing `s` by value transfers ownership into `takes_ownership`, leaving `s` invalid afterward (E0382). Borrowing with `&s` (or calling `s.clone()` if the callee truly needs ownership) keeps `s` alive for the later `println!`.
fj: "Add unsafe — that always works."
fs: "Borrow with `&s`; or clone if the callee must own the value."

q: Why does the compiler reject this borrow pattern (E0502)?

opts.t: Vec::push returns a bool, not ()
opts.why: push returns () — the return type is unrelated to the borrow error.

opts.t: first must be declared mutable to index a Vec
opts.why: Indexing for reading never requires a mutable reference.

opts.t: An immutable borrow of v lives until println!; push needs a mutable borrow — they overlap (E0502)
Vec indexing always clones so there's no borrow at all
opts.why: Indexing yields a reference, not a clone, so the borrow is real.

why: The immutable borrow `first` stays alive until its last use (`println!`). `v.push` needs a mutable borrow in between — Rust forbids overlapping mutable and immutable borrows (E0502). Fix: use `first` before pushing, or copy the value out: `let first = v[0];`.
fj: "Just reorder the lines — order doesn't matter to the borrow checker."
fs: "The borrow extends to its last use; push needs exclusive access — they can't overlap."

q: HashMap get + insert causes a double-mutable-borrow. Pick the idiomatic fix.

opts.t: Use map.entry("hits").or_insert(0)
Clone the map before get, insert into the clone
opts.why: Cloning the whole map just to insert one key is wasteful and misses the point.

opts.t: Wrap map in RefCell to defer borrow checks to runtime
opts.why: RefCell adds runtime overhead and still panics on simultaneous borrows.

opts.t: Use unsafe to bypass the borrow check
opts.why: unsafe doesn't fix the logical race; the entry API is the safe, zero-cost solution.

why: The `entry` API takes exactly one mutable borrow for the entire check-and-insert operation, avoiding the double-borrow. `entry(key).or_insert(default)` inserts the default only when the key is absent — idiomatic, efficient, and compile-safe.
fj: "Just use contains_key then insert — two lines, easy."
fs: "entry().or_insert() is one borrow, one lookup — that's the Rust way."

q: E0515: returning a reference to a local. Which fix compiles?

opts.t: Change return type to &'static str and use a literal
opts.why: Only works if the value is truly static; a heap-allocated String can't be 'static.

opts.t: Add #[allow(dangling_references)] to suppress the error
opts.why: No such lint exists; E0515 is a hard compiler error, not a warning.

opts.t: Return an owned String instead of a reference
Wrap &s in Box::new(&s) to heap-allocate the reference
opts.why: Boxing a reference doesn't extend the referenced value's lifetime; `s` still drops.

why: `s` is a local that is dropped when the function returns; returning `&s` would be a dangling reference (E0515). The fix is to return an owned `String` — transfer ownership to the caller rather than borrowing a value that no longer exists.
fj: "Just add a lifetime annotation 'a and it'll work."
fs: "Local variables die at the closing brace; give the caller ownership instead of a borrow."

q: Mutating a Vec while iterating over it — which statement is correct?

opts.t: The for loop holds an immutable borrow of v; push needs a mutable borrow — they overlap (E0502)
push is not available on Vec<i32>; use append instead
opts.why: push works fine on Vec<i32>; the error is about borrow conflicts, not the method.

opts.t: x must be dereferenced before the loop, not inside it
opts.why: Dereferencing inside the loop is valid; the problem is the concurrent borrow.

opts.t: for loops always move the collection; use iter() explicitly
opts.why: for x in &v already calls .iter() implicitly; the borrow conflict is the real issue.

why: The `for x in &v` loop borrows `v` immutably for its entire duration. `v.push(...)` inside the loop requests a mutable borrow — Rust disallows the overlap (E0502). Fix: collect indices or values first, then mutate after the loop.
fj: "Use iter_mut() and it'll let you push inside the loop."
fs: "Collect what you need first, then push — you can't hold a shared borrow while mutating."

q: Rc<T> across threads — compiler rejects it. What's the correct fix?

opts.t: Wrap Rc in a Mutex to make it Send
opts.why: Mutex<Rc<T>> is still not Send because Rc's non-atomic ref count is inside the Mutex.

opts.t: Replace Rc with Arc (atomic reference counting)
Mark the thread closure with #[allow(non_send)]
opts.why: No such attribute exists; Send is a marker trait enforced at compile time.

opts.t: Clone the Vec and move the clone — Rc is unnecessary here if you clone
opts.why: Cloning the Vec works only if you don't need shared ownership; the question asks for the Rc fix.

why: `Rc<T>` uses non-atomic reference counting and explicitly implements `!Send`, so it cannot be moved across thread boundaries. `Arc<T>` (atomic reference counted) uses atomic operations for its count and is `Send + Sync`, making it the drop-in replacement for multi-threaded scenarios.
fj: "Just add unsafe impl Send for Rc<Vec<i32>>."
fs: "Rc = single-thread only; swap to Arc the moment a thread boundary appears."

q: Propagating errors idiomatically — which version is most idiomatic Rust?

opts.t: Version A — unwrap is cleaner and avoids verbose Result types
opts.why: unwrap() panics on bad input; it's only acceptable in tests or provably-safe one-liners.

opts.t: Version B — ? propagates the error to the caller instead of panicking
Both are equivalent; the compiler optimises unwrap into ?
opts.why: They have different behavior: unwrap panics, ? returns Err to the caller.

opts.t: Neither; use match on the Result to handle both arms explicitly
opts.why: match is valid but verbose for simple propagation; ? is the idiomatic shorthand.

why: The `?` operator desugars to an early `return Err(e.into())` on failure, propagating the error to the caller without panicking. Version B lets callers decide how to handle parse failures; Version A crashes the process on bad input, which is rarely appropriate in library or production code.
fj: "unwrap() is fine — the input is always valid in practice."
fs: "Use `?` to propagate; reserve unwrap for tests or provably-safe values."

q: Does this compile? If not, what's wrong?

opts.t: Compile error: cannot shadow a non-mut binding
opts.why: Shadowing is always legal regardless of mutability; it creates a new binding.

opts.t: Compiles; prints 13
Compiles; prints 12
opts.why: x starts 5, shadow → 6, shadow → 12, then +=1 → 13.

opts.t: Compile error: type mismatch after shadowing
opts.why: All operations stay i32; no type change occurs here.

why: Shadowing creates a new binding each time — it is not mutation. The second `let x` shadows the first (x=6), the third shadows that (x=12) and adds `mut`. The `x += 1` then mutates the third binding to 13. Shadowing lets you reuse a name and even change type without `mut`.
fj: "Shadowing and mutation are the same — both need mut."
fs: "Shadowing = new binding; mutation = write to existing binding. They're distinct."

q: Which statement best explains the compile error here?

opts.t: greet expects &str but received String (type mismatch)
String doesn't implement the + operator with &str
opts.why: String does implement Add<&str>; that's used inside greet itself.

opts.t: owned was already borrowed as s, so it's consumed
opts.why: The shared borrow `s` would prevent a move if still live, but the real error is a type mismatch.

opts.t: No error — String coerces to &str automatically
opts.why: Deref coercion works for &String → &str, not String → &str (owned vs reference).

why: `greet` expects a `&str` (a reference slice), but `owned` is a `String` (heap-owned value). Passing `owned` directly tries to pass an owned `String` where a `&str` is expected. Fix: pass `&owned` or `owned.as_str()` to trigger the deref coercion from `&String` to `&str`.
fj: "String and &str are basically the same — the compiler auto-converts."
fs: "String owns heap data; &str borrows a slice. Coercion needs an explicit &."

q: What does this print — or does it panic at runtime?

opts.t: Compile error: RefCell borrows checked at compile time
opts.why: RefCell moves borrow checks to runtime; this compiles fine.

opts.t: Panics at runtime with 'already borrowed'
Prints [1, 2, 3] because borrow_mut() upgrades b1
opts.why: There is no borrow upgrade; borrow_mut panics if any borrow is outstanding.

opts.t: Undefined behaviour — RefCell has no runtime check
opts.why: RefCell always checks; it panics rather than producing UB.

why: `RefCell<T>` defers borrow checking to runtime using a counter. `borrow()` increments the shared count; `borrow_mut()` panics if that count is nonzero (thread: 'already borrowed: BorrowMutError'). This is a common trap when `RefCell` is used inside structs — keep borrows narrow-scoped.
fj: "RefCell is just like Mutex but cheaper — both silently wait."
fs: "RefCell panics on conflicting borrows; drop shared borrows before calling borrow_mut."

q: The iterator chain looks correct but produces wrong output. Why?

opts.t: Compile error: filter and map have incompatible types
opts.why: filter and map chain fine over &&i32 / &i32; types are compatible.

opts.t: Correct — all even values are printed before 'done'
opts.why: No values are printed; collect() or for-loop is required to drive the iterator.

opts.t: Iterator adapters are lazy; nothing executes until consumed
filter sees &&i32 so the modulo always sees a pointer, never the value
opts.why: The double-deref *x corrects for &&i32; the real issue is laziness, not types.

why: Rust iterators are lazy — `filter` and `map` return new iterator structs that do no work until driven by a consumer (`collect`, `for`, `sum`, `count`, etc.). Without a consumer, the chain is built but never executed, so nothing prints. Drop `.collect::<Vec<_>>()` at the end to materialise results.
fj: "Iterator chains run eagerly like Python list comprehensions."
fs: "Adapters build a recipe; consumers bake it. No consumer → no work done."

q: This async server handler will starve the tokio executor. Why?

opts.t: thread::sleep in an async fn is fine — tokio parks the thread
opts.why: tokio does NOT park threads on thread::sleep; it blocks the entire worker thread.

opts.t: blocking_db_query holds the tokio worker thread for 2s, preventing other tasks from running on that thread
The async fn must be marked #[tokio::main] to avoid starving
opts.why: #[tokio::main] marks the entry point, not individual handlers; it does not fix blocking calls.

opts.t: No issue — tokio spawns a new OS thread per blocked task automatically
opts.why: Only tokio::task::spawn_blocking does that; regular async tasks share a fixed thread pool.

why: Tokio's async runtime multiplexes many tasks over a small thread pool. A blocking call like `thread::sleep` (or blocking I/O) holds a worker thread hostage — no other tasks on that thread can progress. Fix: wrap blocking work in `tokio::task::spawn_blocking(|| blocking_db_query(id)).await` to run it on a dedicated blocking thread pool.
fj: "async fn means it's non-blocking by definition."
fs: "async fn cooperates only at .await points; blocking calls starve the worker — use spawn_blocking."

q: This tokio service will deadlock under concurrent load. Where?

opts.t: std::Mutex is not async-aware; holding it across an .await point can deadlock
static Mutex cannot be unwrapped; use once_cell instead
opts.why: Mutex::new in a static is valid; the deadlock comes from holding it across await.

opts.t: sleep is not a yield point in tokio — use yield_now() instead
opts.why: sleep does yield, but the guard is still held across that yield, which causes the deadlock.

opts.t: No issue — std::Mutex is safe to hold across await in a single-threaded runtime
opts.why: Even on a single-threaded runtime this causes a deadlock when the same task tries to re-lock.

why: Holding a `std::sync::Mutex` guard across an `.await` point means: task A holds the lock, suspends at `sleep`, task B on the same thread tries to acquire the same lock — deadlock. Fix: use `tokio::sync::Mutex` whose `lock().await` is async-aware and correctly suspends without blocking the thread, or release the guard before awaiting.
fj: "std::Mutex works fine in async — it's just a normal lock."
fs: "std::Mutex blocks the thread; tokio::sync::Mutex yields. Never hold std guards across .await."

q: This code leaks memory silently. What's the root cause?

opts.t: RefCell borrow_mut panics when Rc count > 1
opts.why: RefCell tracks borrows independently of Rc count; this doesn't panic here.

opts.t: a and b form a reference cycle; Rc can't detect cycles so neither is ever dropped
Rc<RefCell<Node>> is not Send so the OS reclaims memory on drop
opts.why: !Send means it can't cross thread boundaries; it doesn't affect memory reclamation on the same thread.

opts.t: Node must derive Drop to be freed by Rc
opts.why: Rc calls the default Drop automatically; a custom Drop is not required.

why: `a` points to `b` and `b` points back to `a`. Each `Rc` keeps a reference count; because both counts stay ≥ 1 forever, neither node is ever dropped — a classic reference cycle. Fix: break the cycle with `Weak<T>` for back-pointers (`Rc::downgrade`). `Weak` doesn't increment the strong count, so the cycle is broken.
fj: "Rc has a garbage collector in the background — it'll clean up cycles."
fs: "Rc is not a GC; use Weak for back-edges to avoid cycle leaks."

q: unsafe transmute — what invariant is violated?

opts.t: transmute changes the Vec length metadata incorrectly
opts.why: as_slice() gives a fat pointer; transmute just reinterprets types — length stays correct here.

opts.t: This particular input is valid UTF-8 (0x41=A, 0x42=B) but the pattern is unsound for arbitrary bytes
transmute between reference types is always undefined behaviour in Rust
opts.why: transmute between &[u8] and &str compiles; the UB risk is specifically the UTF-8 invariant.

opts.t: v must be &'static to transmute into &str
opts.why: Lifetime is not the issue; UTF-8 validity is the invariant str requires.

why: `&str` has a hard invariant: it must be valid UTF-8. `transmute` bypasses the check — if `v` contained non-UTF-8 bytes, subsequent string operations would produce undefined behaviour (e.g., `chars()`, `format!`). Here [0x41, 0x42] = "AB" so it happens to work, but the code is unsound. Use `std::str::from_utf8(v.as_slice())?` to get a `Result<&str, Utf8Error>` safely.
fj: "transmute is just a cast — safe as long as sizes match."
fs: "&str must be valid UTF-8; transmute skips that check. Use from_utf8 and handle the error."

q: A crate publishes this public async API. What's the ecosystem-level flaw?

opts.t: async fn in a public API is not stable — use impl Future instead
opts.why: async fn in trait (not impl) had restrictions pre-1.75, but async fn in plain impl is fine.

opts.t: Client is not Send because Rc<Inner> is not Send; any caller that spawns Client on a tokio task gets a compile error
fetch should return Result<String, Error> not String
opts.why: That's a design preference; it doesn't cause an ecosystem-level breakage for async runtimes.

opts.t: Rc<Inner> causes a double-free if Client is cloned
opts.why: Rc's clone increments refcount safely; no double-free occurs.

why: Publishing a `Client` with `Rc<T>` inside makes `Client: !Send`. Any downstream crate using `tokio::spawn(async { client.fetch(...).await })` gets a compile error because `tokio::spawn` requires `Send`. This is a **semver-compatible but ecosystem-breaking** change that forces callers to restructure concurrency. Library authors must use `Arc<T>` (or document the single-thread constraint explicitly) when shipping public async types.
fj: "Rc is faster than Arc — internal fields don't matter for the public API."
fs: "Public async types MUST be Send; internal Rc leaks !Send to every downstream spawn site."

q: This trait change ships in a minor version bump. Is it semver-breaking?

opts.t: Not breaking — adding a default method to a trait is always backwards-compatible
opts.why: It is breaking when downstream crates implement the trait AND have their own inherent method named validate — the new default conflicts.

opts.t: Breaking — downstream impl blocks that already define validate as an inherent method will get an ambiguity error
opts.why: Inherent methods shadow trait defaults without error; the real breakage is more subtle.

opts.t: Potentially breaking — if a downstream crate implements Processor and ALSO defines validate via another trait, the new default creates an ambiguity compile error
Not breaking — default methods are opt-in; downstream ignores them unless called
opts.why: Ambiguity errors can arise at call sites or blanket-impl overlap regardless of whether the method is 'called'.

why: Adding a default method to a public trait is a **major semver hazard** in Rust. If a downstream crate implements `Processor` and another trait in scope also provides `validate`, the compiler may emit an ambiguity error — breaking their build with no action on their part. The Rust API guidelines classify this as a **minor-but-breaking** change; bump to 2.0.0 or use a sealed-trait pattern to prevent external implementations.
fj: "Default methods are backwards-compatible by definition — it's just sugar."
fs: "Adding defaults to public traits can break downstream via trait-ambiguity; treat it as a major bump."

q: This library function is in a hot path. What production risk does it carry?

opts.t: No risk — expect is equivalent to unwrap_or_default in release builds
opts.why: expect always panics on Err in all build profiles; it is NOT equivalent to unwrap_or_default.

opts.t: Panics unwind through the caller's stack, potentially corrupting shared state if caught
opts.why: Rust panics by default unwind cleanly; corruption is not the primary concern for library code.

opts.t: A panic in a library function crashes the entire binary — callers cannot recover without catch_unwind; it should return Result
std::env::var is not safe to call in async contexts
opts.why: env::var is a blocking syscall but very fast; the real issue is the panic, not async context.

why: Panicking library code is an **anti-pattern at ecosystem scale**: callers can't distinguish a missing config from a bug, can't retry or provide a fallback, and in an FFI or `catch_unwind` boundary the panic becomes undefined behaviour. Return `Result<String, VarError>` and let the application layer decide the abort policy. Reserve `expect`/`unwrap` for truly-unrecoverable invariants internal to the library.
fj: "expect documents the invariant — it's better than returning Result."
fs: "Library panics are uncatchable without catch_unwind; always return Result from public APIs."

q: A crate registers a custom global allocator. What ecosystem risk does this create?

opts.t: No risk — #[global_allocator] is scoped to the crate that declares it
opts.why: #[global_allocator] is process-wide, not crate-scoped.

opts.t: The allocator conflicts if a downstream binary also sets #[global_allocator], causing a linker error
jemalloc is incompatible with tokio's async allocator, causing memory corruption
opts.why: tokio doesn't define its own allocator; jemalloc + tokio is a common and valid combination.

opts.t: Performance risk only — jemalloc is slower than the system allocator for small allocs
opts.why: Performance is debatable; the correctness risk (linker conflict) is the real ecosystem hazard.

why: `#[global_allocator]` is **process-wide and unique** — only one can exist per binary. A library crate that sets it prevents any binary or other library from doing the same; the linker emits 'symbol already defined'. This is a **critical library anti-pattern**: libraries must never set `#[global_allocator]`. Only the final binary (application crate) should control the allocator. Document this as a known limitation or use `#[cfg(feature = "jemalloc")]` to let the application opt-in.
fj: "Setting the allocator in a lib crate is an optional hint — binaries can override it."
fs: "#[global_allocator] is unique per binary; libraries must never set it or they break dependents."
